title: EXPOSÉ VI A GENERALITIES ON ALGEBRAIC GROUPS

pages.title: EXPOSÉ VI A GENERALITIES ON ALGEBRAIC GROUPS

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ VIA
GENERALITIES ON ALGEBRAIC GROUPSby P. Gabriel

Throughout this chapter, A will denote an artinian local ring with residue field \(k\). A group scheme G over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\) will simply be called an \(A\)-group. This \(A\)-group is said to be locally of finite type if the underlying scheme is locally of finite type over A; it is said to be algebraic if the underlying scheme is of finite type over A.
0. Preliminary remarks

Let us first consider a group scheme G over an arbitrary scheme S. We call multiplication the structural morphism \(\mu : G\times_S G \to G\) and inversion the morphism \(c : G \to G\) defined by the equalities \(c(T)(x) = x^{-1}\) (T being a scheme over S and \(x\) an element of \(G(T)\)). If U and V are subsets of the underlying set of G, we denote by \(U\cdot V\) the image under the multiplication morphism of the subset of \(G\times_S G\) consisting of the points whose first projection belongs to U and whose second to V. Likewise, the notations \(U^{-1}\) and \(c(U)\) are equivalent.

Let \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) be the projection of \(G\times_S G\) onto the first factor and \(\sigma : G\times_S G \to G\times_S G\) the morphism with components \(\mathrm{pr}_1\) and \(\mu\). For every S-scheme T, \(\sigma(T)\) is the map \((x,y) \mapsto (x,xy)\); it follows that \(\sigma\) is an automorphism. The composite of this automorphism and of the projection \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) of \(G\times_S G\) onto the second factor is the multiplication morphism. When G is flat over S, \(\mathrm{pr}_2\), hence \(\mu\), are flat morphisms; when G is smooth over S, \(\mathrm{pr}_2\), hence \(\mu\), are smooth morphisms, etc.

We assume from now on that S is the spectrum of an artinian local ring A with residue field \(k\). We denote by \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) the category of reduced schemes over \(k\). For every scheme X over A, the reduced scheme \(X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is an object of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) and the functor \(X \mapsto X_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is right adjoint to the inclusion of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/A)\). It follows that, for every \(A\)-group G, \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a group in the category \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\).

Conversely, if \(k\) is a perfect field, the inclusion of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) in \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)\) commutes with products, so that the groups in the category \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) are identified with the group schemes over \(k\) whose underlying scheme is reduced. In particular, for every \(k\)-group G, \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a group subscheme of G; this subgroup is not normal in G in general: for example, if \(k\) is a field of characteristic 3, the constant group \((\mathbb{Z}/(2))_k\) acts in a nontrivial way on the diagonalizable group \(\mathbf{D}_k(\mathbb{Z}/(3))\) (I.4.1 and I.4.4); if G denotes the semidirect product of \(\mathbf{D}_k(\mathbb{Z}/(3))\) by \((\mathbb{Z}/(2))_k\) defined by this action, \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is identified with \((\mathbb{Z}/(2))_k\) and is not normal in G.

If \(k\) is an arbitrary field and H a group in the category \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), then \((H\otimes_k k^{p^{-\infty}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a group scheme over \(k^{p^{-\infty}}\). Since \(H\otimes_k k^{p^{-\infty}}\) has the same underlying topological space as \((H\otimes_k k^{p^{-\infty}})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\), we see that the groups of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) share with \(k\)-groups certain topological properties that are invariant under extension of the base field: for example, it will follow from 0.3 and the remarks just made that every group of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is separated. We shall encounter in the sequel groups of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) whenever we deal with a nonempty locally closed subset U of an \(A\)-group G such that \(U\cdot U = U\) and \(U^{-1} = U\): indeed, the reduced subscheme of G defined by U is a group of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\).

An \(A\)-group G is always separated: indeed, the diagonal of \(G\times_A G\) is identified with the functor from \((\mathrm{Sch}/A)^{\circ}\) with values in \((\mathrm{Ens})\) which associates with every scheme S over A the inverse image of the identity element of \(G(S)\) under the map \(\varphi(S) : (x,y) \mapsto xy^{-1}\) from \(G(S)\times G(S)\) to \(G(S)\). We consequently have a cartesian square \[ \xymatrix{ G\times_A G \ar[r]^-{\varphi} & G \\ G \ar[u]^-{\text{diagonal morphism}} \ar[r] & \mathrm{Spec}\ A \ar[u]_-{\text{unit section}} } \] so that the diagonal morphism, being deduced from a closed immersion by base change, is itself a closed immersion.

Let G be an \(A\)-group. We shall say that a point \(g\) of G is strictly rational over A if there exists an \(A\)-morphism \(s : \mathrm{Spec}\ A \to G\) sending the unique point of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\) to \(g\). One knows that such an \(s\) defines an automorphism \(r_s\) of the scheme G over A, called the right translation by \(s\): for every morphism \(\pi : S \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\), \(r_s(S)\) is the automorphism \(x \mapsto x\cdot(G(\pi)(s))\).
Likewise, we denote by \(\ell_s\) the left translation by \(s\), that is, the automorphism of G defined by the equalities \((\ell_s(S))(x) = (G(\pi)(s))\cdot x\).

Since \(G\otimes_A k\) and G have the same underlying topological space \(\overline{G}\), since \(G\otimes_A k\) is a \(k\)-group and since \(s\otimes_A k\) depends only on \(g\) and not on \(s\), we see that the automorphisms of \(\overline{G}\) induced by \(r_s\) and \(\ell_s\) (or by \(r_{s\otimes k}\) and \(\ell_{s\otimes k}\)) depend only on \(g\) and not on \(s\); when P is a subset of \(\overline{G}\), we may therefore write \(P\cdot g\) and \(g\cdot P\) instead of \(r_s(P)\) and \(\ell_s(P)\), which is in accordance with 0.1.

Let us now consider two everywhere dense open subsets U and V of an \(A\)-group G locally of finite type. The product \(U\cdot V\) (cf. 0.1) is then equal to the whole space underlying G: indeed, since G and \(G\otimes_A k\) have the same underlying space, we may assume, replacing A by \(k\) and G by \(G\otimes_A k\) if necessary, that A is a field \(k\). In this case the multiplication morphism is flat and locally of finite type (cf. 0.1), hence open, so that \(U\cdot V\) is an open subset of G. If \(\overline{k}\) is an algebraic closure of \(k\), we obviously have \((U\cdot V)\otimes_k\overline{k} = (U\otimes_k\overline{k})\cdot(V\otimes_k\overline{k})\); by SGA 1 VIII §4, it therefore suffices to prove that \(G\otimes_k\overline{k} = (U\otimes_k\overline{k})\cdot(V\otimes_k\overline{k})\), and we are reduced to the case where \(k\) is algebraically closed. In this last case, it suffices to prove that \(U\cdot V\) contains every closed point \(x\) of G; if \(k\) is algebraically closed, such an \(x\) is strictly rational, and the everywhere dense open sets \(U^{-1}\cdot x\) and V have a closed point \(v\) in common; if \(u\) is the element of \(G(k)\) defined by the equality \(x = u\cdot v\), then \(u^{-1}\cdot x\) belongs to \(U^{-1}\cdot x\), so that \(u\) belongs to U, which proves the assertion.

pages.title: 1. Local properties of an \(A\)-group locally of finite type

blocks.html: 1. Local properties of an \(A\)-group locally of finite type
We shall first see that, if G is locally of finite type and flat over A, one can “make every closed point of G strictly rational” by means of a finite flat extension of the base.

Unless expressly stated otherwise, we shall assume from now on that G is an \(A\)-group locally of finite type. When A is a field, we shall obtain in exposé VIIB very precise results on the local rings of G. Here we content ourselves with a few elementary results:

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.1.1

Let \(x\) be a point of an \(A\)-group G locally of finite type and flat over A. Then the local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) is Cohen-Macaulay and there exists a system of parameters \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) of \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}/(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\) is a flat \(A\)-module.

We first assume A equal to its residue field \(k\); it then suffices to prove that \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) is Cohen-Macaulay, and we may restrict to the case where \(x\) is a closed point (cf. EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\), 16.5.13). By lemma 1.1.2 below, G contains a closed point \(y\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{G,y}\) is Cohen-Macaulay. By SGA 1, I §9, this amounts to saying that, for every finite extension K of the base field \(k\) and every point \(\overline{y}\) of \(\overline{G} = G\otimes_k K\) above \(y\), \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{G},\overline{y}}\) is Cohen-Macaulay. If the extension K has been chosen large enough (i.e. if K contains the normal extension of \(k\) generated by the residue fields \(k(y)\) and \(k(x)\)), \(\overline{y}\) is (strictly) rational over K, as is every point \(\overline{x}\) of \(\overline{G}\) above \(x\). Since the automorphism \(r_{\overline{x}}\circ(r_{\overline{y}})^{-1}\) maps \(\overline{y}\) to \(\overline{x}\), it follows that \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{G},\overline{x}}\), hence \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) (SGA 1, I §9), are Cohen-Macaulay.

When A is again assumed arbitrary, the preceding reasoning applies to \(k\otimes_A G\), so that \(k\otimes_A\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) is Cohen-Macaulay. If \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) is a sequence of elements of \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) whose image in \(k\otimes_A\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\) is a system of parameters, it follows from SGA 1, IV 5.7 or from EGA \(0_{\mathrm{IV}}\), 15.1.16, that \(a_1,\ldots,a_n\) is an \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}\)-regular sequence and that \(\mathscr{O}_{G,x}/(a_1,\ldots,a_n)\) is finite and flat over A.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 1.1.2

Every nonempty scheme X, locally of finite type over an artinian ring A, contains a closed point \(x\) whose local ring is Cohen-Macaulay.

We may obviously assume X affine with algebra B and argue by induction on \(\dim X\) (the assertion is clear if X is discrete, all the local rings then being artinian). Since B is of finite type over A, if \(\dim B > 0\), B contains an element \(a\) which is not invertible and not a zero divisor; the closed subscheme \(X' = \mathrm{Spec}\ B/(a)\) of X is then of dimension strictly less than \(\dim X\) and, by the induction hypothesis, contains a closed point \(x\) such that \(\mathscr{O}_{X',x}\) is Cohen-Macaulay. Since \(\mathscr{O}_{X',x} = \mathscr{O}_{X,x}/(a)\) and \(a\) is not invertible and not a zero divisor in \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\), \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is Cohen-Macaulay (see also EGA \(\mathrm{IV}_2\), 6.11.3).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.2

Let A be an artinian ring, G an \(A\)-group locally of finite type and flat over A, and \(x\) a closed point of G. Then there exists a local \(A\)-algebra \(A'\), finite and free over A, such that every point of \(G\otimes_A A'\) above \(x\) is strictly rational over \(A'\).

Indeed, one knows that there exists a local \(A\)-algebra \(A_1\), finite and free over A and such that the residue field of \(A_1\) contains the normal extension of \(k\) generated by the residue field \(k(x)\) of \(x\). In this case, all the points \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) of \(G\otimes_A A_1\) above \(g\in G\) have the same residue field as \(A_1\) (i.e. \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) are rational over \(A_1\) in the sense of V, §4; e)). So let \(B_1,\ldots,B_n\) be the local rings of \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\). By 1.1.1, \(B_1,\ldots,B_n\) possess quotients \(B'_1,\ldots,B'_n\) which are artinian and flat over \(A_1\). If we set \(A' = B'_1\otimes_{A_1}\cdots\otimes_{A_1}B'_n\), the algebras \(B_i\otimes_{A_1}A'\) are all local and they have a quotient isomorphic to \(A'\), so that \(A'\) answers the question.

Let \(e\) be the identity element (or origin) of G, that is, the image of the unique point of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\) under the unit section \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A \to G\). By the very definition, \(e\) is strictly rational over A.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 1.3.1

Let G be a group locally of finite type and flat over an artinian ring A, and K the perfect closure of the residue field \(k\) of A. The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(G\otimes_A K\) is reduced.

  2. (i bis)

    If \(\mathscr{O}_{G,e}\) is the local ring of the origin of G, \(\mathscr{O}_{G,e}\otimes_A K\) is reduced.

  3. (ii)

    G is smooth over A.

  4. (ii bis)

    G is smooth over A at the origin.

By means of SGA 1, II.2.1, we reduce at once to the case where A is a field \((A = k)\). The implications (i)⇒(i bis), (ii)⇒(ii bis), (ii)⇒(i) and (ii bis)⇒(i bis) are obvious, so that it suffices to prove that (i bis) implies (ii). Now, if \(\overline{k}\) denotes the algebraic closure of \(k\), it follows from (i bis) that \(\mathscr{O}_{G,e}\otimes_k\overline{k}\) is reduced. Moreover, if \(x\) is an arbitrary closed point of \(\overline{G} = G\otimes_k\overline{k}\), there is exactly one \(\overline{k}\)-morphism \(s : \mathrm{Spec}\ \overline{k} \to G\otimes_k\overline{k}\) whose image is \(x\); the right translation \(r_s\) then induces an isomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{G,e}\otimes_k\overline{k}\) onto \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{G},x}\), so that \(\mathscr{O}_{\overline{G},x}\), hence \(\overline{G}\), are reduced. But, since \(\overline{G}\) is also locally of finite type over \(\overline{k}\), \(\overline{G}\) contains at least one point \(y\) closed in \(\overline{G}\) and smooth over \(\overline{k}\). Since, on the other hand, the local rings of the closed points of \(\overline{G}\) are all isomorphic to \(\mathscr{O}_{G,e}\otimes_k\overline{k}\), we see that all these local rings are regular, so that \(\overline{G}\) is smooth over \(\overline{k}\), hence G smooth over \(k\).

pages.title: 2. Connected components of an \(A\)-group locally of finite type

blocks.html: 2. Connected components of an \(A\)-group locally of finite type

Let us first consider an arbitrary \(A\)-group G and let \(G'\) be the connected component of the origin \(e\) of G. This connected component is obviously closed, so that we may identify it with the reduced closed subscheme of G which has \(G'\) as underlying space.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.1.1

For every extension K of the residue field \(k\) of A, \(G'\otimes_A K\) has as underlying space the connected component of the origin in the K-group \(G\otimes_A K\) (\(G'\) is geometrically connected).

Indeed, let \((G\otimes_A K)'\) be the connected component of the origin in \(G\otimes_A K\). Since the image of \((G\otimes_A K)'\) in G is connected and contains the identity element of G, this image is contained in \(G'\), so that \((G\otimes_A K)'\) is contained in the inverse image \(G'\otimes_A K\) of \(G'\) in \(G\otimes_A K\). The proposition thus follows from the connectedness of \(G'\otimes_A K\), which is proved in lemma 2.1.2:

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.2

Let X and Y be two connected schemes over a field \(k\). If X contains a rational point, \(X\times_k Y\) is connected.

We give below a direct proof of this result of EGA \(\mathrm{IV}_2\) (4.5.8 and 4.5.14). Suppose first that Y is nonempty, connected and affine with algebra B. In this case, \(X\times_k Y\) is the spectrum of the quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra \(\mathscr{B} = \mathscr{O}_X\otimes_k B\). We want to show that every subset U of \(X\times_k Y\) which is open, closed and nonempty coincides with \(X\times_k Y\). And indeed U is affine over X and its affine \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra is a direct factor of \(\mathscr{B}\). It thus follows from lemma 2.1.3 below that the image of U in X is open and closed, i.e. coincides with all of X. This image contains in particular a rational point \(x\) of X, so that U meets the inverse image of \(x\) in \(X\times_k Y\). Since the latter is isomorphic to Y, hence connected, U contains this inverse image. The same result would hold for the complement of U in \(X\times_k Y\) if U were distinct from \(X\times_k Y\), which would be absurd.

If Y is now an arbitrary \(k\)-scheme, the preceding shows that the fibres of the canonical projection \(X\times_k Y \to Y\) are connected. If \(x\) is a rational point of X, these fibres all meet the subscheme \(\{x\}\times_k Y\), which is itself connected, whence the proposition.

blocks.title: Lemma
blocks.html:
Lemma 2.1.3

Let X be a scheme and \(\mathscr{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Algebra which is a direct factor of a free \(\mathscr{O}_X\)-Module. The image of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\) in X is then open and closed.

Let V be this image. It is clear that V is contained in the support of \(\mathscr{A}\). Conversely, if \(x\) belongs to the support of \(\mathscr{A}\), \(\mathscr{A}_x\) is nonzero and is a free \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\)-module (Kaplansky). Consequently the fibre of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\) at \(x\), which is affine with algebra \(\mathscr{A}_x\otimes_{\mathscr{O}_{X,x}}k(x)\), is not empty. We thus see that the image of \(\mathrm{Spec}\ \mathscr{A}\) coincides with the support of \(\mathscr{A}\). If \(s\) is the unit section of \(\mathscr{A}\), the equality \(s_x = 0\) implies that \(s\) vanishes in a neighbourhood of the point \(x\), so that the support of \(\mathscr{A}\) is closed. On the other hand, we may assume that \(\mathscr{A}\) is a direct factor of the direct sum of a family \((\mathscr{O}_X^{\alpha})\) of copies of \(\mathscr{O}_X\); let then \(\varphi^{\alpha}\) be the restriction to \(\mathscr{A}\) of the canonical projection of \(\coprod_{\alpha}\mathscr{O}_X^{\alpha}\) onto \(\mathscr{O}_X^{\alpha}\); if \(x\) is a point of X, every homomorphism of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\)-modules \(\psi_x : \mathscr{A}_x \to \mathscr{O}_{X,x}\) is of the form \(a \mapsto \sum_{\alpha}\xi^{\alpha}\varphi^{\alpha}(a)\) where \((\xi^{\alpha})\) is a family of elements of \(\mathscr{O}_{X,x}\). Since \(\mathscr{A}_x\) is free and nonzero, we can choose the \(\xi^{\alpha}\) and \(a\) in such a way that \(1 = \sum_{\alpha}\xi^{\alpha}\varphi^{\alpha}(a) = \xi^{\alpha_1}\varphi^{\alpha_1}(a)+\cdots+\xi^{\alpha_n}\varphi^{\alpha_n}(a)\). The equality \(1 = \xi^{\alpha_1}\varphi^{\alpha_1}(a)+\cdots+\xi^{\alpha_n}\varphi^{\alpha_n}(a)\) then remains valid in a neighbourhood of \(x\), which shows that \(a\), hence \(\mathscr{A}\), are nonzero in a neighbourhood of \(x\) (“the support of a projective module is open”).

The notation still being that of 2.1, it is clear that \(G'\) is a reduced scheme over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ k \subset \mathrm{Spec}\ A\). Lemma 2.1.2 shows that \(G'\times_k G'\) is connected, so that \((G'\times_k G')_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is the reduced subscheme of \(G\times_A G\) whose underlying space is the connected component of the origin. In particular, the multiplication morphism \(\mu : G\times_A G \to G\) induces a morphism \(\mu' : (G'\times_k G')_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \to G'\) which makes \(G'\) a group in the full subcategory of \((\mathrm{Sch}/k)\) consisting of the reduced \(k\)-schemes.

In accordance with our conventions of 1.1, we again assume from now on that G is locally of finite type over A. Then G is locally noetherian, hence locally connected, so that the connected component of the identity element is open in G. We shall denote by \(G^0\) the subscheme induced by G on this open set, so that with the notation of 2.1 we have \(G' = (G^0)_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\). It obviously follows from 2.2 that \(G^0\) is a group subscheme of G, which we shall call the identity component of G.

Let \(G^{\alpha}\) be an arbitrary connected component of G and \(\nu^{\alpha} : G^{\alpha}\times_A G^0 \to G\) the morphism defined by the equalities \((\nu^{\alpha}(S))(g,\gamma) = g\gamma g^{-1}\) (\(S\in(\mathrm{Sch}/A)\), \(g\in G^{\alpha}(S)\), \(\gamma\in G^0(S)\)). If \(e\) is the origin of G, the restriction of \(\nu^{\alpha}\) to \(G^{\alpha}\times_A\{e\}\) is the null morphism; since \(G^{\alpha}\times_A G^0\) is connected by 2.1.2, we see that \(\nu^{\alpha}\) factors through \(G^0\), so that \(G^0\) is a normal \(A\)-subgroup of G (for the preceding implies that, for every scheme S over A, \(G^0(S)\) is a normal subgroup of \(G(S)\)).

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 2.4

The identity component of an \(A\)-group G locally of finite type is geometrically irreducible and is of finite type over A.

Let \(\overline{k}\) be the algebraic closure of the residue field \(k\) of A, and let us first show that \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible, that is, that \(G^0\otimes_A\overline{k}\) is irreducible. By 2.2, \((G\otimes_A\overline{k})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is a \(\overline{k}\)-group locally of finite type and reduced, hence smooth over \(\overline{k}\) (1.3.1). A fortiori the local rings of \((G\otimes_A\overline{k})_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) are integral domains, so that the connected components of \(G\otimes_A\overline{k}\), in particular \(G^0\otimes_A\overline{k}\), are irreducible.

Let us now show that \(G^0\) is of finite type over A. Since \(G^0\) is locally of finite type over A, it suffices to prove that \(G^0\) is quasi-compact. For this, let V be an affine open subset of \(G^0\). Since \(G^0\) is irreducible, this open set is dense in \(G^0\), so that \(V\cdot V\) coincides with \(G^0\) (0.5); since \(V\cdot V\) is the image of \(V\times_A V\) under the multiplication morphism, \(V\cdot V\) is quasi-compact.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 2.4.1

Every connected component of G is irreducible and of finite type over A.

Indeed, we may assume A equal to its residue field \(k\). Let then H be a connected component of G, \(x\) a closed point of H, \(k(x)\) the residue field of \(x\), and K the normal extension of \(k\) generated by \(k(x)\). The canonical projection \(\pi : H\otimes_k K \to H\) is open and closed, so that every connected component of \(H\otimes_k K\) is the sum of the images of \(G^0\otimes_k K\) under the translations defined by the points of \(\pi^{-1}(x)\). Since \(H\otimes_k K\) is of finite type, so is H. If H were not irreducible, one could choose \(x\) in such a way that \(x\) belongs to two distinct irreducible components. The local ring \(\mathscr{O}_{H,x}\) would then have two distinct minimal prime ideals, as would the local ring of every point of \(H\otimes_k K\) above \(x\) (the “going down theorem” of Cohen-Seidenberg, cf. Bourbaki, Alg. comm. chap. VI); yet we have just seen that \(H\otimes_k K\) is the direct sum of a certain number of copies of \(G^0\otimes_k K\), which is irreducible...

pages.title: 3. Construction of quotient groups (the case of groups of finite type)

blocks.html: 3. Construction of quotient groups (the case of groups of finite type)

Let A be an artinian local ring and \(u : F \to G\) a homomorphism of \(A\)-groups. If \(\mu : F\times_A F \to F\) and \(\nu : G\times_A G \to G\) denote the multiplication morphisms and \(\lambda\) the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ F\times_A G \ar[r]^-{u\times G} & G\times_A G \ar[r]^-{\nu} & G, } \] one recalls that the left quotient \(F\backslash G\) of G by F is the cokernel of the \((\mathrm{Sch}/A)\)- groupoid \(G_*\) described below: \[ \xymatrix{ F\times_A F\times_A G \ar@<1.2ex>[r]^-{F\times\lambda} \ar[r]|-{\mu\times G} \ar@<-1.2ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_{2,3}} & F\times_A G \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\lambda} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & G } \] (\(\mathrm{pr}_2\) and \(\mathrm{pr}_{2,3}\) are the projections of \(F\times_A G\) and \(F\times_A(F\times_A G)\) onto the second factors). We shall say that \(G_*\) is the groupoid with base G defined by u (confer V, 2a; as in exposé V, we do not follow in VIA the convention of IV, 4.6.15).

Since the unique \(A\)-morphism \(F \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\) is universally open (EGA \(\mathrm{IV}_2\), 2.4.9), \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is an open morphism; the same therefore holds for \(\lambda\), which is the composite of \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) and of the automorphism \(\sigma\) of \(F\times_A G\) defined by the following formulas: \(\sigma(S)(x,y) = (x,u(S)(x)\cdot y)\), where S is a variable \(A\)-scheme, \(x\) and \(y\) belonging to \(F(S)\) and \(G(S)\). One sees in the same way that \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) and \(\lambda\) are flat when F is flat over A.

Let us also remark, to conclude these preliminaries, that every \(A\)-morphism \(s : \mathrm{Spec}\ A \to G\) defines an automorphism of the groupoid \(G_*\) which induces on G, \(F\times_A G\) and \(F\times_A F\times_A G\) the automorphisms \(r_s\), \(\mathrm{id}_F\times_A r_s\) and \(\mathrm{id}_F\times_A\mathrm{id}_F\times_A r_s\), respectively. We shall again denote by \(r_s\) this automorphism of \(G_*\) and we shall say that \(r_s\) is the right translation defined by \(s\) (confer 0.4).

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem 3.2

Let F and G be groups flat and locally of finite type over an artinian local ring A. Let \(u : F \to G\) be a quasi-compact homomorphism of \(A\)-groups with finite kernel. Then:

  1. (i)

    The left quotient \(F\backslash G\) of G by F exists and the sequence \[ \xymatrix{ F\times_A G \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\lambda} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & G \ar[r]^-{\text{canonical}} & F\backslash G } \] is exact in the category of all ringed spaces.

  2. (ii)

    The canonical morphism \(G \to F\backslash G\) is surjective and open, and \(F\backslash G\) is a direct sum of schemes of finite type over A.

  3. (iii)

    The canonical morphism \(F\times_A G \to G\times_{(F\backslash G)}G\) is surjective.

  4. (iv)

    If \(u\) is a monomorphism, \(F\times_A G \to G\times_{(F\backslash G)}G\) is an isomor- phism and \(G \to F\backslash G\) is flat.

In the proof of this theorem, \(A'\) will denote a local \(A\)-algebra, finite and free over A. If R is a relation involving \(A'\), we shall say that \(R(A')\) is true when \(A'\) is large enough if there exists an algebra \(A_1\), local, finite and free over A, such that the relation \(R(A')\) holds for every algebra \(A'\) which is local, finite and free over \(A_1\). We shall first prove our theorem when F and G are of finite type over A:

Suppose for a moment that every point of G possesses an open and saturated neighbourhood W such that the groupoid induced by \(G_*\) on W possesses a quasi-section (cf. V §6). Theorem 3.2 then follows from V 6.1. We shall show that, in addition, every finite subset of \(F\backslash G\) is then contained in an affine open set.

If U is a quasi-section of the groupoid induced by \(G_*\) on a saturated open subset W of G, then \(U\otimes_A A'\) is indeed a quasi-section of the \((\mathrm{Sch}/A')\)-groupoid induced by \(G_*\otimes_A A'\) on \(W\otimes_A A'\). Moreover, if \(U_*\) is the \((\mathrm{Sch}/A)\)-groupoid induced by \(G_*\) on U, \(U_*\otimes_A A'\) is identified with the \((\mathrm{Sch}/A')\)-groupoid induced by \(G_*\otimes_A A'\) in \(U\otimes_A A'\). It thus follows from the proofs of exposé V that the construction of the quotient \(X = F\backslash G\) commutes with the extension \(A \to A'\) of the base of the type considered here.

So let \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\) be points of \(F\backslash G\), which we may assume closed, and \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) closed points of G projecting to \(x_1,\ldots,x_n\); let U be an everywhere dense affine open subset of \(F\backslash G\), V the inverse image of U in G, and let \(A'\) be an \(A\)-algebra large enough for the needs of our cause: then the inverse image of \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) in \(G\otimes_A A'\) consists of points \(g'_1,\ldots,g'_p\) which are strictly rational over \(A'\) (1.2); moreover, if \(A'\) is large enough, the everywhere dense open set \(\bigcap_i(V\otimes_A A')^{-1}\cdot g'_i\) contains a point \(x\) strictly rational over \(A'\). We thus have \(x\in(V\otimes_A A')^{-1}\cdot g'_i\), whence \(g'_i\in(V\otimes_A A')\cdot x\). Since \((V\otimes_A A')\cdot x\) is the image of \(V\otimes_A A'\) under an automorphism of the groupoid \(G_*\otimes_A A'\), the image \((U\otimes_A A')\cdot x\) of \((V\otimes_A A')\cdot x\) in \((F\backslash G)\otimes_A A'\) is the image of \(U\otimes_A A'\) under the automorphism induced on \((F\backslash G)\otimes_A A'\). Hence \((U\otimes_A A')\cdot x\) is an affine open subset of \((F\backslash G)\otimes_A A'\) containing \(g'_1,\ldots,g'_p\).

To finish the proof, one shows as in V §5.b) that \((U\otimes_A A')\cdot x\) contains an affine open set \(W'\) which contains \(g'_1,\ldots,g'_p\) and which is saturated for the equivalence relation \[ \xymatrix{ (F\backslash G)\otimes_A A'\otimes_A A' \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & (F\backslash G)\otimes_A A' } \] induced by the two canonical injections of \(A'\) into \(A'\otimes_A A'\). The image of \(W'\) in \(F\backslash G\) then contains \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) and is an affine open subset of \(F\backslash G\) (V 4.1).

For every algebra \(A'\) over A (of the type considered in 4.1), let us now denote by \(U(A')\) the set of points of \(G\otimes_A A'\) having an open and saturated neighbourhood W such that the groupoid induced by \(G_*\otimes_A A'\) on W possesses a quasi-section. It is quite clear that \(U(A')\) is saturated for the operations of \(G(\mathrm{Spec}\ A')\) on \(G\otimes_A A'\). We shall see that, when \(A'\) is large enough, \(U(A')\) is equal to \(G\otimes_A A'\):

The proof is by induction on \(\dim(G-U(A))\): let \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) be closed points belonging to the various irreducible components of \(G-U(A)\); if \(A'\) is large enough, the points \(g'_1,\ldots,g'_p\) of \(G\otimes_A A'\) projecting to \(g_1,\ldots,g_n\) are strictly rational over \(A'\); likewise, \(U(A)\otimes_A A'\) contains a strictly rational point \(x\) (\(U(A)\) is nonempty by V 8.1). Consequently, \(U(A')\) contains \((U(A)\otimes_A A')\cdot x^{-1}g'_i\) for every \(i\); hence \(U(A')\) contains \(g'_1,\ldots,g'_p\) and we have \[ \dim(G\otimes_A A' - U(A')) < \dim(G-U(A)). \] The induction hypothesis then implies the existence of an algebra \(A''\) over \(A'\) such that \(U(A'') = (G\otimes_A A')\otimes_{A'}A'' = G\otimes_A A''\).

We are now in a position to prove the existence of \(F\backslash G\) when F and G are of finite type over A: let \(A'\) be large enough over A for \(U(A')\) to coincide with \(G\otimes_A A'\) (confer 3.2.2). We shall set \(A'' = A'\otimes_A A'\) and, for every \(A\)-scheme X, we shall denote by \(X'\) and \(X''\) the fibre products \(X\otimes_A A'\) and \(X\otimes_A A''\). By 3.2.1 and 3.2.2, the quotients \(F'\backslash G'\) and \(F''\backslash G''\) exist and we have the commutative diagram

\[ \xymatrix{ F''\times_{A''} G'' \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\lambda''} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2''} \ar@<0.6ex>[d]^-{w_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[d]_-{w_2} & G'' \ar[r]^-{p''} \ar@<0.6ex>[d]^-{v_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[d]_-{v_2} & F''\backslash G'' \ar@<0.6ex>[d]^-{u_1} \ar@<-0.6ex>[d]_-{u_2} \\ F'\times_{A'} G' \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\lambda'} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2'} \ar[d]_-{h} & G' \ar[r]^-{p'} \ar[d]^-{g} & F'\backslash G' \\ F\times_A G \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\lambda} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & G & } \]

In this diagram, \(\mathrm{pr}_2'\) and \(\lambda'\) (resp. \(\mathrm{pr}_2''\) and \(\lambda''\)) are obtained from \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) and \(\lambda\) by obvious base changes; the morphisms \(g\) and \(h\) are induced by the canonical injection \(A \to A'\). There, \(p'\) and \(p''\) denote the canonical morphisms; the morphisms \(v_1,v_2\) and \(w_1,w_2\) are induced by the two canonical injections of \(A'\) into \(A''\); finally, if \(\pi' : F'\backslash G' \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A'\) is the structural morphism, it follows from the fact that the construction of the quotient \(F'\backslash G'\) commutes with the two base changes \(f_1, f_2 : \mathrm{Spec}\ A'' \rightrightarrows \mathrm{Spec}\ A'\) that we have canonical isomorphisms \(i_1\) and \(i_2\) of \(F''\backslash G''\) onto \((F'\backslash G')\times_{\pi',f_1}\mathrm{Spec}\ A''\) and \((F'\backslash G')\times_{\pi',f_2}\mathrm{Spec}\ A''\).

Now, when one has a diagram of type (∗), \(\mathrm{Coker}(\mathrm{pr}_2,\lambda)\) exists if and only if the same holds for \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_1,u_2)\), and these two cokernels are identified. The existence of \(F\backslash G\) will thus follow from that of \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_1,u_2)\). Now it follows from the compatibility of the formation of \(F\backslash G\) with the base extensions considered here that the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ (F'\backslash G')\times_{\pi',f_1}\mathrm{Spec}\ A'' \ar[r]^-{i_1^{-1}} & F''\backslash G'' \ar[r]^-{i_2} & (F'\backslash G')\times_{\pi',f_2}\mathrm{Spec}\ A'' } \] is a descent datum on \(F'\backslash G'\) relative to \(f : \mathrm{Spec}\ A' \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\). By SGA VIII §7.6 and 3.2.1 this descent datum is effective, that is to say, \(\mathrm{Coker}(u_1,u_2)\) exists (one could moreover use V 4.1 directly).

To finish the proof of theorem 3.2 in the case where F and G are of finite type over A, it remains to study the quotient \(F\backslash G\). By V 6.1, assertions (ii), (iii) and (iv) “become true” after the base change \(f : \mathrm{Spec}\ A' \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\); by EGA \(\mathrm{IV}_2\), 2.6.1, 2.6.2 and 2.7.1, these assertions were therefore true before the base change. Finally, to prove the second assertion of (i), one need only refer to V §6.c).
4. Construction of quotient groups (general case)
We now assume the hypotheses of theorem 3.2 satisfied, F and G not necessarily being of finite type over A.

Let us first of all consider a connected component \(G^{\alpha}\) of G and show that the saturation \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}\) of \(G^{\alpha}\) for the equivalence relation defined by the groupoid \(G_*\) is an open and closed subset of G (in other words, is the union of certain connected components of G). This saturation is the image of \(F\times_A G^{\alpha}\) under \(\lambda\), hence is open in G (confer §3.1). If \(k\) is the residue field of A and \(\overline{k}\) an algebraic closure of \(k\), it remains to show that the image of \((F\times_A G^{\alpha})\otimes_A k\) under \(\lambda\otimes_A k\) is closed in \(G\otimes_A k\), or again, by SGA 1, VIII.4.4, that the image of \((F\times_A G^{\alpha})\otimes_A\overline{k}\) under \(\lambda\otimes_A\overline{k}\) is closed. Since \(G^{\alpha}\otimes_A\overline{k}\) is the union of a finite number of connected components of \(G\otimes_A\overline{k}\), we are reduced to the case where A is an algebraically closed field, which we shall assume: in this case, \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}\) is the union of the images of \(G^{\alpha}\) under the left translations \(\ell_{u(x)}\), where \(x\) runs through the closed points of F; the assertion thus follows from the fact that these images are connected components of G.

Let us take in particular for \(G^{\alpha}\) the connected component \(G^0\) of the origin of G. Then \(\overline{G^0}\) obviously contains the image of F under \(u\), which is none other than the equivalence class of the origin. On the other hand, if \(F^{\beta}\) is a connected component of F, \(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0\) is connected (2.1.2), so that the image of \(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0\) under \(\lambda\) is contained in the connected component of \(u(F^{\beta})\) in G. In other words, \(\overline{G^0}\) is the union of the connected components which meet the image of F.

It will also be noted that the open subscheme of G having \(\overline{G^0}\) as underlying space is a subgroup of G (which we again denote by \(\overline{G^0}\)): indeed, the inversion morphism of G preserves the image of F and permutes the connected components of G which meet this image; it thus suffices to show that \(\nu : G\times_A G \to G\) maps \(\overline{G^0}\times_A\overline{G^0}\) into \(\overline{G^0}\), and for this we may assume that A is an algebraically closed field (with the notation of 4.1, \(\overline{G^0}\otimes_A\overline{k}\) is indeed identified with the saturation of \((G\otimes_A\overline{k})^0\) for the equivalence relation defined by the homomorphism \(u\otimes_A\overline{k}\)); if \(G^{\gamma}\) and \(G^{\delta}\) are then connected components of \(\overline{G^0}\), \(G^{\gamma}\times_A G^{\delta}\) is connected and its image under \(\nu\) meets the image of F; consequently, \(u(G^{\gamma}\times_A G^{\delta})\) is contained in a connected component of G meeting \(u(F)\), q.e.d.

It follows from the preceding that the groupoid \(G_*\) with base G defined by \(u\) is the direct sum of the groupoids \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}_*\) induced by \(G_*\) on the various open and closed subsets of G of the form \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}\). The cokernel of \(G_*\) is therefore the direct sum of the cokernels of these groupoids \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}_*\), which one is led to study separately. Let us first of all consider the groupoid \(\overline{G^0}_*\) induced by \(G_*\) on \(\overline{G^0}\). It is clear that \(\overline{G^0}_*\) is the groupoid with base \(\overline{G^0}\) defined by the homomorphism of F into \(\overline{G^0}\) induced by \(u\) (§3.1). The cokernel whose existence we want to prove is thus identified with \(F\backslash\overline{G^0}\). Let us consider on the other hand the groupoid \[ \xymatrix{ G^0_2 \ar@<1.2ex>[r]^-{\ell_2'} \ar[r]|-{\ell_1'} \ar@<-1.2ex>[r]_-{\ell_0'} & G^0_1 \ar@<0.8ex>[r]^-{\ell_1} \ar@<-0.8ex>[r]_-{\ell_0} & G^0_0=G^0 } \] induced by \(\overline{G^0}_*\) on \(G^0\). If one refers to the construction made explicit in V §3.b), the object denoted there \(Y_0\times_{X_0}X_1\) is none other than \(F\times_A G^0\), so that \(G^0_1\) is the inverse image of \(G^0\) under the morphism \(F\times_A G^0 \to G^0\) induced by \(\lambda\). I claim that this inverse image is \(F_0\times_A G^0\), if we denote by \(F_0\) the inverse image of \(G^0\) under \(u\): indeed, if \(F^{\beta}\) is a connected component of \(F_0\) and \(e\) the identity element of G, \(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0\) is connected (2.1.2) and \(\lambda(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0)\) is contained in \(G^0\); conversely, if \(F^{\beta}\) is a connected component of F not contained in \(F_0\), the image of \(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0\) is again connected and contains \(u(F^{\beta})\); if \(u(F^{\beta})\) is not contained in \(G^0\), \(\lambda(F^{\beta}\times_A G^0)\) does not meet \(G^0\).

It follows from the preceding that the groupoid \(G^0_*\) induced by \(G_*\) on \(G^0\) is the groupoid with base \(G^0\) defined by the homomorphism \(F_0 \to G^0\) induced by \(u\): by paragraph 4, this groupoid possesses a cokernel, which is none other than \(F_0\backslash G^0\). I now claim that \(F_0\backslash G^0\) is identified with \(F\backslash\overline{G^0}\): indeed, the proof is analogous to that of assertion (i) of lemma V §6.1; let us consider the diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ \overline{G^0} & F\times_A G^0 \ar[l]_-{v} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & G^0, } \] where \(v\) is the morphism induced by \(\lambda\). Since \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) possesses a section, \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) is an effective and universal epimorphism, so that \(F_0\backslash G^0\) coincides with \(\mathrm{Coker}(v_0,v_1)\), where

is the inverse image of the groupoid \(G^0_*\) under \(\mathrm{pr}_2\) (cf. V §3.a)), that is to say also the inverse image of \(\overline{G^0}_*\) under the composite morphism \[ \xymatrix{ F\times_A G^0 \ar[r]^-{\text{inclusion}} & F\times_A\overline{G^0} \ar[r]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} & \overline{G^0}. } \] Likewise, since \(v\) is faithfully flat and quasi-compact, \(F\backslash\overline{G^0}\) coincides with the cokernel of the inverse image of the groupoid \(\overline{G^0}_*\) under the base change \(v\). Now this inverse image is isomorphic to \(V_*\) by V §3c); it follows from this that the canonical inclusion of \(G^0_*\) in \(\overline{G^0}_*\) induces an isomorphism of \(F_0\backslash G^0\) onto \(F\backslash\overline{G^0}\). We note finally that: the construction of \(F\backslash\overline{G^0}\) commutes with finite and locally free base changes, because the same holds for \(F_0\backslash G^0\).

It remains to construct the cokernel of the groupoid \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}_*\) when \(G^{\alpha}\) is an arbitrary connected component of G: if \(A'\) is a local \(A\)-algebra, finite and free and large enough (confer 3.2), \(G^{\alpha}\otimes_A A'\) is the union of a finite number of connected components \(G'_1,\ldots,G'_n\) of \(G\otimes_A A'\), all of which possess a strictly rational point. For every \(i\), there thus exists a right translation \(r_i\) of \(G\otimes_A A'\) which maps \(G^0\otimes_A A'\) onto \(G'_i\); this translation induces an isomorphism of the groupoid \(\overline{G^0}_*\otimes_A A'\) onto \((\overline{G'_i})_*\), so that the groupoid induced by \(G_*\otimes_A A'\) on the saturation of \(G'_i\) possesses a cokernel. Since \((\overline{G^{\alpha}})_*\otimes_A A'\) is the direct sum of a certain number of the \((\overline{G'_i})_*\), \((\overline{G^{\alpha}})_*\otimes_A A'\) possesses a cokernel; this cokernel is the direct sum of a certain number of copies of \((F_0\otimes_A A')\backslash(G^0\otimes_A A')\), so that every finite subset of this cokernel is contained in an affine open set; moreover, the construction of this cokernel commutes with finite and locally free extensions of the base. One thus sees as in 3.2.3 that this cokernel is of the form \(Y\otimes_A A'\), where Y is a cokernel of \((\overline{G^{\alpha}})_*\).

We have thus constructed \(F\backslash G\). The other assertions of theorem 3.2 reduce directly to assertions concerning the groupoids \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}_*\). As in V §6, it suffices to prove (ii), (iii) and (iv); by SGA VIII it suffices to verify the corresponding assertions for the groupoid \(\overline{G^{\alpha}}_*\otimes_A A'\), which is isomorphic to the direct sum of a finite number of copies of \(\overline{G^0}_*\otimes_A A'\) (confer 4.4), so that we are reduced to the groupoid \(\overline{G^0}_*\). For the latter one continues to copy the proof established in V §6 as we began to do in 4.3.

Let us add, to conclude this paragraph, a few remarks concerning lemma 6.1 and §9.a) of exposé V: with the hypotheses and the notation of V §9.a), we look for a condition under which the construction of the cokernel of the \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)-groupoid \(X_*\) commutes with an extension \(\pi : S' \to S\) of the base. Since the cokernels of \(X_*\) and \(X'_*\) are identified with the cokernels of the groupoids \(U_*\) and \(U'_*\) induced by \(X_*\) and \(X'_*\) on the quasi-sections U and \(U'\), we are reduced to the case of a \((\mathrm{Sch}/S)\)- groupoid satisfying the hypotheses of theorem V 4.1. If \(g : U_0 \to Y\) is the canonical morphism of \(U_0=U\) onto the cokernel Y of \(U_*\), we want the sequence of \(\mathscr{O}_{Y'}\)-Modules \[ (*)\qquad \mathscr{O}_{Y'} \longrightarrow g'_*(\mathscr{O}_{U_0'}) \rightrightarrows (gu_1)'_*(\mathscr{O}_{U_1'}) = (gu_0)'_*(\mathscr{O}_{U_1'}) \] to be exact. If S is locally noetherian and \(X_0\) of finite type over S, or if \((d_0,d_1)\) is an equivalence pair, it follows from EGA \(\mathrm{III}_1\), 1.4.5 that the sequence (∗) is identified with the inverse image of the sequence \[ (**)\qquad \mathscr{O}_Y \longrightarrow g_*(\mathscr{O}_{U_0}) \rightrightarrows g_*u_{1*}(\mathscr{O}_{U_1}) = g_*u_{0*}(\mathscr{O}_{U_1}) \] whenever \(S'\) is flat over S. Since (∗∗) is an exact sequence, we see that the construction of the cokernel of \(X_*\) commutes with flat extensions of the base under the hypotheses mentioned.

Let us now consider the case of the groupoid \(G_*\) of theorem 3.2 when F and G are provisionally assumed of finite type over A. By 3.2.2 there exists an algebra \(A'\), local, finite and free over A, such that the groupoid \(G_*\otimes_A A'\) possesses quasi-sections “locally”. For every extension \(T \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\) of the base, the sequence \[ \xymatrix{ (F''\backslash G'')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T \ar@<0.6ex>[r] \ar@<-0.6ex>[r] & (F'\backslash G')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T \ar[r] & (F\backslash G)\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T } \] deduced from diagram (∗) of 3.2.3 is exact. If moreover T is assumed flat over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\), \((F''\backslash G'')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) and \((F'\backslash G')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) are identified respectively with the cokernels of \((G_*\otimes_A A'')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) and \((G_*\otimes_A A')\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\). The diagram deduced from (∗), 3.2.3 by the base change \(T \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\) then shows that \((F\backslash G)\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) is identified with the cokernel of \(G_*\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\). An analogous reasoning is valid in the general case:

Under the hypotheses of theorem 3.2, for every flat \(A\)-scheme T, \((F\backslash G)\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) is identified with the left quotient of \(G\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\) by \(F\times_{\mathrm{Spec}\ A}T\).
5. Complements

We resume the notation of §3 and the hypotheses of theorem 3.2; we then have the following commutative diagram \[ \xymatrix{ F\times_A G\times_A G \ar[r]^-{F\times\nu} \ar@<0.8ex>[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2\times G} \ar@<-0.8ex>[d]_-{\lambda\times G} & F\times_A G \ar@<0.8ex>[d]^-{\mathrm{pr}_2} \ar@<-0.8ex>[d]_-{\lambda} \\ G\times_A G \ar[r]^-{\nu} \ar[d]_-{\mathrm{can.}\times G} & G \ar[d]^-{\mathrm{can.}} \\ (F\backslash G)\times_A G \ar@{-->}[r]^-{\rho} & F\backslash G, } \] which satisfies the equalities \(\mathrm{pr}_2\circ(F\times\nu) = \nu\circ(\mathrm{pr}_2\times G)\) and \(\lambda\circ(F\times\nu) = \nu\circ(\lambda\times G)\). Moreover, since G is assumed flat over A, the left-hand vertical sequence is exact by 4.7, so that \(\nu\) induces a morphism of \(A\)-schemes: \[ \rho : (F\backslash G)\times_A G \longrightarrow F\backslash G. \] This morphism \(\rho\) makes G act on the right on \(F\backslash G\), as one verifies immediately; moreover, the canonical morphism \(G \to F\backslash G\) commutes with the right actions of G on G and \(F\backslash G\).

When the homomorphism of \(A\)-groups \(u : F \to G\) is a monomorphism, one can recover 5.1 by using the results of exposé IV. Indeed, the canonical morphism \(p : G \to F\backslash G\) is faithfully flat and open by 3.2; it is therefore covering for the (fpqc) topology (IV 6.3.1) and one can apply the corollaries IV.5.2.2 and IV.5.2.4. In particular, if we assume, in addition to the hypotheses of 3.2, that \(u\) is the inclusion in G of a normal subgroup F, there exists on \(F\backslash G\) one and only one structure of \(A\)-group such that the canonical morphism \(p : G \to F\backslash G\) is a homomorphism of \(A\)-groups.
We shall now review a few statements of exposé IV:

The statements IV 5.2.7 and IV 5.3.1 translate as follows: let F and G be two groups locally of finite type and flat over A, F being a closed normal subgroup of G. The maps \(H \mapsto F\backslash H\) and \(H' \mapsto H'\times_{(F\backslash G)}G\) define a one-to-one correspondence between the flat \(A\)-subgroups of G containing F and the flat \(A\)-subgroups of \(F\backslash G\). In this bijection the closed (resp. normal) subgroups of G containing F correspond to the closed (resp. normal) subgroups of \(F\backslash G\).

Proposition IV 5.2.9 implies the following result: let F, H and G be groups locally of finite type and flat over A; suppose F contained and closed in G, H contained in G and containing F, and F normal in H. Under these conditions, \(F\backslash H\) acts freely on the left on \(F\backslash G\), the quotient scheme \((F\backslash H)\backslash(F\backslash G)\) exists and we have a canonical isomorphism of schemes with group of operators G: \[ (F\backslash H)\backslash(F\backslash G) = H\backslash G. \]

From IV 5.2.8, finally, follows the assertion below: let F, H and G be groups locally of finite type and flat over A; suppose that F is contained, closed and normal in G, that H is contained in G and that \(F\cap H\) is flat over A. Let then \(F\rtimes_A H\) be the \(A\)-group whose underlying scheme is the product \(F\times_A H\), the multiplication being defined by the morphism “\(((x,h),(y,h'))\mapsto(xhyh^{-1},hh')\)”; likewise, let \(u : H\cap F \to F\rtimes_A H\) be the monomorphism \(x \mapsto (x^{-1},x)\) and \(F\cdot H\) the quotient \((F\cap H)\backslash(F\rtimes_A H)\). Under these conditions there exists a canonical isomorphism \[ F\backslash(F\cdot H) = (F\cap H)\backslash H. \]

Let \(u : F \to G\) be a quasi-compact homomorphism of groups locally of finite type and flat over A; suppose that the kernel of \(u\) is also flat over A. In this case, the quotient group \((\mathrm{Ker}\ u)\backslash F\) exists by theorem 3.2 and 5.2; moreover, \(u\) induces a monomorphism \(v : \mathrm{Ker}\ u\backslash F \to G\) (IV 5.2.6) and \(\mathrm{Ker}\ u\backslash F\) is flat over A by 3.2 (iv). On the other hand, \(\mathrm{Ker}\ u\backslash F\) is identified with the quotient sheaf of \(\widehat{F}\) by \(\mathrm{Ker}\ \widehat{u}\) for the (fpqc) topology of \((\mathrm{Sch}/A)\) (confer 5.2); it follows that \(F\backslash G\) is identified with \((\mathrm{Ker}\ u\backslash F)\backslash G\), for the quotient sheaf \(\widehat{F}\backslash\widehat{G}\) for the canonical topology is identified with the quotient sheaf \((\mathrm{Ker}\ \widehat{u}\backslash\widehat{F})\backslash\widehat{G}\). A fortiori \(v\) induces an isomorphism of \((\mathrm{Ker}\ u)\backslash F\) onto the inverse image in G of the distinguished point of \(F\backslash G\) (so that \(v\) is a closed immersion; confer VIB). When F and G are commutative \(A\)-groups, this inverse image is the image of \(u\) in the category of abelian \(A\)-groups, whereas \((\mathrm{Ker}\ u)\backslash F\) is the coimage of \(u\). Whence:

blocks.title: Theorem
blocks.html:
Theorem

The category of commutative groups of finite type over a field is abelian.
Indeed, when A is a field, \(\mathrm{Ker}\ u\) is flat over A whatever \(u\) may be.

Let G be a group locally of finite type and flat over an artinian local ring A. We know that the connected component of the origin \(G^0\) is a normal subgroup of G (2.3). This subgroup \(G^0\) acts on the left on all the connected components \(G^{\alpha}\) of G, so that \(G^0\backslash G\) is the direct sum of the \(G^0\backslash G^{\alpha}\). In particular, the connected component of the origin in \(G^0\backslash G\) is none other than \(G^0\backslash G^0 \cong \mathrm{Spec}\ A\) and \(G^0\backslash G \to \mathrm{Spec}\ A\) is a local isomorphism at the origin; consequently, \(G^0\backslash G\) is unramified over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\) (3.3); since \(G^0\backslash G\) is also flat over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\) by theorem 3.2 (iv), we see that \(G^0\backslash G\) is an étale group over \(\mathrm{Spec}\ A\).

Now let \(k\) be a perfect field and G a group locally of finite type over \(k\). We have seen (0.2) that \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is then a group subscheme of G. Moreover, the equivalence class of the origin of G for the left action of \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) on G is the whole space underlying G. It thus follows from theorem 4.2 that the quotient \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash G\) is the spectrum of a finite local \(k\)-algebra.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition

Let \(u : F \to G\) be a homomorphism of groups locally of finite type over a perfect field \(k\). The following assertions are equivalent:

  1. (i)

    \(u\) is flat.

  2. (ii)

    \(u^0 : F^0 \to G^0\) is dominant and the morphism \(v : F_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash F \to G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash G\) induced by \(u\) is flat.

Indeed, let us consider the following commutative diagram: \[ \xymatrix{ F_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \ar[r]^-{i} \ar[d]_-{u_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}} & F \ar[r]^-{p} \ar[d]^-{u} & F_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash F \ar[d]^-{v} \\ G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}} \ar[r]_-{j} & G \ar[r]_-{q} & G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash G, } \] where \(i\) and \(j\) denote the inclusions, \(p\) and \(q\) the canonical projections. By theorem 3.2 (iv), \(p\) and \(q\) are faithfully flat; consequently, if \(u\) is flat, \(qu=vp\) is flat, hence so is \(v\).

Conversely, if \(v\) is flat, F and G are flat over \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\backslash G\); by SGA 1, IV 5.9 it thus suffices to show that the morphism \(u^{-1}(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}) \to G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) induced by \(u\) is flat; this is true because \(G_{\mathrm{r\acute{e}d}}\) is reduced (3.3.1).
